title: Whittled hazel honey dipper
subtitle: A turned-groove honey dipper in green hazel
excerpt: A honey dipper carved from green hazel with a grooved head that traps honey. The grooves are cut with the sloyd knife using a series of stop cuts and wedge cuts. About 16 cm finished length.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics for single-knife kitchen treen. Honey-dipper form documented in Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) and the Museum of English Rural Life open-access treen archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven wood, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools.
A short straight-bladed knife with a stout handle; the Swedish school-craft standard for whittling and spoon-carving.

content.text: A honey dipper traps honey in a series of carved grooves around its head. When the dipper is rolled between the fingers over a jar, the honey fills the grooves and clings to the surface rather than dripping. Hazel is an excellent material: straight-grained, coppiced readily, carves cleanly when
green
, and dries food-safe. A straight section of hazel rod about 20 cm long and 20 mm in diameter is the starting piece.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

Hazel is one of the most abundant coppice materials in Britain and northern Europe. Cut it in late autumn or early winter when the sap is down and the rod is at its greenest. A 20 mm diameter rod gives the right proportions for a honey dipper. Birch or sycamore rods of the same diameter work just as well if hazel is not available.
Shaping the handle
Use push cuts with the
sloyd knife
to peel the bark from the whole rod. The handle section runs from 7 cm from one end to the other end: about 13 cm. Taper the handle from 18 mm at the head end down to 12 mm at the tip.
Shaping the head

The head occupies the 7 cm at the lower end of the rod. Use push cuts to shape it as a cylinder 20 mm in diameter with a slight dome at the bottom. The dome is cut with push cuts working from the centre of the bottom face outward toward the rim, exactly as shaping the back of a spoon bowl.
Cutting the grooves

The grooves are cut with a sequence of stop cuts and wedge cuts. Mark five evenly spaced lines around the head, each 8 mm apart. At each line, make a stop cut: press the blade straight down into the wood at 90 degrees to the axis, cutting 3 mm deep all the way round the circumference.

Then make a wedge cut on the lower side of each stop cut: angle the blade at 45 degrees into the stop cut and push from 5 mm below the stop cut up to the stop-cut line. The wedge cut and the stop cut together lift a chip, leaving a V-shaped groove around the head.

Repeat for each of the five grooves. The finished head has five ridges and five valleys. Work round the full circumference of each groove before moving to the next one; this keeps the depth consistent.
Finishing

Let the dipper dry in a paper bag for two weeks. Apply board butter: rub it over the whole piece, let it stand twenty minutes, buff off the surplus. Board butter is food-safe from the first application. Re-apply once a month.
Variations
Larger head: use a 25 mm rod and cut eight grooves.
Kolrosing on the handle: inscribe a simple geometric line pattern on the handle using the kolrosing technique and rub in ground coffee.
Care
Rinse under warm water after use, shake off, and let dry upright or hanging. No soaking, no dishwasher. Re-oil when the wood looks dry.

rows.symptom: Grooves are uneven depth around the circumference
rows.cause: Stop cut not taken consistently all the way round before the wedge cut
rows.fix: Make the full circumference stop cut first, then come back for the wedge cut. Do not alternate stop-and-wedge in short arcs.

rows.symptom: Chip breaks off the top ridge rather than lifting cleanly
rows.cause: Wedge cut too steep or too close to the stop cut boundary
rows.fix: The wedge cut starts 5 mm below the stop cut and angles up to meet it. A shallower starting angle (30 degrees rather than 45) gives a cleaner lift.
